text: This book provides a comparative analysis of the political, military, and economic dimensions of the Soviet occupation in Austria, Hungary, and Romania. Drawing on extensive documentation from archives in all three nations as well as Russia, the authors challenge the traditional understanding of Soviet foreign policy during this era. While previous scholarship has focused primarily on the political transition—specifically how and when Stalin decided to impose Bolshevik systems—this work illustrates that the conquest of East-Central Europe was driven by a powerful imperial logic. The Soviet Union viewed these occupied territories not just as political satellites, but as strategic military and economic buffers. Beyond high-level policy, the book also examines the harrowing human cost of the occupation, documenting the atrocities, plunder, mass deportations, and sexual violence that defined the lived experience of the local populations.
